sections.heading: Background: The Pneumatomachi and the Holy Spirit
sections.html:
Background: The Pneumatomachi and the Holy Spirit

Called by Emperor Theodosius I and meeting in Constantinople in AD 381, the First Council of Constantinople was the second ecumenical council. It completed the Trinitarian settlement that Nicaea had begun.

In the fifty years since Nicaea, a new controversy had arisen. While the Nicene Creed had definitively settled the question of the Son's consubstantiality with the Father, it had said almost nothing about the Holy Spirit. A group called the Pneumatomachi ("Spirit-fighters") — also called Macedonians — denied the full deity of the Holy Spirit, treating him as a divine instrument or a created being superior to angels but subordinate to the Son.

Gregory of Nazianzus ("The Theologian"), appointed bishop of Constantinople by Theodosius, led the orthodox party and presided briefly over the council. He had already delivered his celebrated Five Theological Orations (380) defending the full divinity of the Spirit. "What then?" he asked. "Is the Spirit God? Most certainly. Well then, is He consubstantial? Yes, if He is God." Gregory resigned the presidency mid-council due to conflict over his appointment, and Nectarius was chosen to succeed him.

The council also addressed residual Arianism (especially Apollinarianism — the view that Christ had a human body but a divine mind rather than a full human soul).

sections.heading: The Expanded Creed
sections.html:
The Expanded Creed

The council's most enduring legacy is the expansion of the Nicene Creed of 325, producing what is now universally called the Nicene Creed (more precisely, the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed). The expansion chiefly concerned the article on the Holy Spirit, replacing the sparse phrase "And in the Holy Ghost" with a full confession of his deity and work:

And [we believe] in the Holy Ghost, the Lord and Giver of life, who proceedeth from the Father, who with the Father and the Son together is worshipped and glorified, who spake by the prophets.

The phrase "who with the Father and the Son together is worshipped and glorified" was theologically decisive. Worship (proskunesis) and glorification were reserved for God alone; to say the Spirit receives co-equal worship with the Father and Son was to confess his full deity without using the word homoousios — a strategic choice given ongoing controversy over that term.

The phrase "who proceedeth from the Father" echoes John 15:26. The later Western addition "and from the Son" (filioque) was not part of the original creed and became a major point of contention in the Great Schism of 1054. (John 15:26; Acts 2:33; Rom. 8:11)

The creed also expanded the article on the Son, adding "was crucified also for us under Pontius Pilate; he suffered and was buried; and the third day he rose again according to the Scriptures; and ascended into heaven" — drawing on the language of 1 Cor. 15:3–4.

sections.heading: The Canons
sections.html:
The Canons

The council issued four canons of enduring significance:

Canon 1. The faith of the three hundred and eighteen Fathers assembled at Nicaea in Bithynia shall not be set aside, but shall remain firm. And every heresy shall be anathematized — particularly that of the Eunomians [Anomoeans], that of the Arians or Eudoxians, that of the Semi-Arians or Pneumatomachi, and that of the Sabellians, Marcellians, Photinians, and Apollinarians.

Canon 2. The bishops are not to go beyond their own diocese for ordinations or any other ecclesiastical administrations. Diocesan boundaries established by the holy Fathers at Nicaea shall be maintained.

Canon 3. The Bishop of Constantinople, however, shall have the prerogative of honour after the Bishop of Rome; because Constantinople is New Rome. — Note: this canon established Constantinople's honor among the patriarchates and was contested by Rome.

Canon 4. Regarding Maximus the Cynic and the disorder he caused at Constantinople: what has been done by him or for him is declared null and void. He was never a bishop, nor are those ordained by him clergy of any rank.

The council's condemnation of Apollinarianism in Canon 1 rested on Gregory of Nazianzus's theological axiom: "What has not been assumed has not been healed." If Christ did not take on a full human soul, then the human soul is not redeemed by the Incarnation. The full humanity of Christ — body, soul, and rational mind — was essential to his saving work. (Heb. 2:14–17; Heb. 4:15; John 1:14)
